title: Public Service Rage: Government Workers Who Saw Too Much
dek: You sit at the metal desk under fluorescent lights, processing forms while the system fails the people you were hired to protect. The rage doesn't live in your mind; it settles in your psoas, tightens your masseter, and burns in your gut as you witness the machinery of bureaucracy grind down human lives.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Architecture of Institutional Betrayal


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T00:13:18.239Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

You are sitting in a chair that was purchased by a committee twelve years ago, staring at a screen that refreshes with the sluggish indifference of a dying animal. It is 3:14 PM on a Tuesday in November. Outside, the sky is the color of wet concrete, but you haven't seen the sky since you parked your car in the employee lot at dawn. You are a government worker. You are a public servant. You are a witness to the slow, grinding machinery of a system that was ostensibly designed to help people, but which functions, in practice, as a meat grinder for human hope.

The rage you feel is not the hot, explosive anger of a sudden insult. It is a cold, dense, sedimentary rage. It accumulates in your body, layer by layer, form by form, denial by denial. You feel it in the levator scapulae, the muscles that hike your shoulders up toward your ears as if you are bracing for a blow that never lands but is always threatened. You feel it in the deep, structural tension of your psoas, the muscle of fight or flight, which is constantly engaged but never allowed to execute its evolutionary mandate. You cannot fight the bureaucracy. You cannot flee your pension plan. So you sit, and you absorb.

When you took this job, you believed in the mission. You believed in the social contract. You thought you would be the one to hand out the life preservers. Instead, you are the one who has to explain, day after day, that the life preservers are on backorder, that the applicant filled out form 8-B instead of 8-C, that the deadline passed yesterday, that the system is down, that the rules are the rules. You watch people break down in front of you, or on the phone, and you have to maintain the neutral, professional tone of an agent of the state. You swallow the bile. You swallow the grief. You swallow the rage.

This swallowing is a physical act. It requires the constriction of the throat, the tightening of the diaphragm, the suppression of the breath. Over time, this somatic holding pattern becomes your default posture. Your body forgets how to be soft. Your fascia, the connective tissue that wraps every muscle and organ, becomes rigid, glued together by the constant drip of cortisol and adrenaline. You go home at night, and your body smells like stale coffee, recycled air, and the sour sweat of chronic, low-grade panic.

## The Somatic Cost of Bearing Witness

In Traditional Chinese Medicine, anger is associated with the Liver meridian. The Liver is responsible for the smooth flow of Qi, or life energy, throughout the body. When anger is suppressed, when it is not allowed to move and express itself, the Liver Qi stagnates. This stagnation manifests physically: tension in the neck and shoulders, headaches behind the eyes, digestive issues, a feeling of a lump in the throat. You know this lump. You have carried it for years. It is the unsaid apologies, the unexpressed outrage, the screams you swallowed while explaining a policy you knew was unjust.

Bessel van der Kolk, in his seminal work [Herb Pharm Stress Manager (1 oz)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005JW1F1C?tag=spankyspinola-20), explains that trauma is not just an event that happens in the past; it is the imprint left by that experience on mind, brain, and body. As a public servant, you are experiencing secondary trauma, vicarious trauma, institutional trauma. You are the shock absorber for a society that refuses to care for its most vulnerable. The stories you hear, the desperation you witness, the systemic cruelty you are forced to enact - these do not just pass through you. They lodge in your tissues. They change your biology.

You might try to numb it. You might drink a glass of wine, or three, when you get home. You might scroll mindlessly through your phone until your eyes burn. You might tell yourself that you are just doing your job, that you are one of the good ones, that you are making a difference from the inside. But your body knows the truth. Your body knows that you are complicit in a system that causes harm, even as you try to mitigate that harm. This cognitive dissonance is a physical tearing. It is a war between your values and your actions, fought on the battlefield of your own nervous system.

Consider the masseter, the thick muscle of the jaw. It is one of the strongest muscles in the human body, designed for crushing and grinding. When you are forced to smile at a supervisor who is implementing a cruel new directive, when you are forced to speak in the measured, bureaucratic language of denial, your masseter tightens. You grind your teeth at night. You wake up with a jaw so sore it hurts to chew. This is not a dental problem. This is a somatic expression of unvoiced fury. This is the body trying to chew through the bars of its own cage.

## The Herbal Allies for Stagnant Rage

You cannot simply think your way out of this. You cannot rationalize the tension out of your fascia. You need allies that speak the language of the body, allies that can move the stagnant energy and soften the rigid tissues. This is where the plants come in. Herbs are not just pleasant flavors; they are complex biochemical messengers that interact with our nervous systems, our endocrine systems, our very cells.

When the Liver Qi is stagnant, when the rage is hot and stuck, we look to the bitter and cooling herbs. We look to dandelion root, burdock, Oregon grape root. These herbs stimulate the liver, encouraging it to process and release the metabolic and emotional waste that has accumulated. But for the specific, exhausted rage of the public servant, we also need nervines - herbs that nourish and support the nervous system.

I often recommend [Gaia Herbs Calm A.S.A.P.](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07H2QFMNR?tag=spankyspinola-20) for this kind of chronic, depleting stress. Ashwagandha is an adaptogen, meaning it helps the body adapt to stress and restores balance. It doesn't numb the rage; it gives the nervous system the resilience to hold the rage without breaking. It supports the adrenal glands, which have been pumping out cortisol since you first realized the system was broken. It helps to shift the body from the sympathetic 'fight or flight' state to the parasympathetic 'rest and digest' state.

You take the tincture. You taste the earthy, slightly bitter flavor. You feel it travel down your throat, a message of support from the earth to your exhausted body. You are not alone. The plants are with you. They have survived for millions of years by adapting to harsh environments, by finding ways to thrive in the cracks of the concrete. They offer you this wisdom. They offer you their strength.

## The Wisdom of the Ancestors and the Somatic Lineage

The rage you feel is not just your own. It is the accumulated rage of generations of people who have been crushed by indifferent systems. It is the rage of the ancestors. Resmaa Menakem, in [FES Pink Yarrow](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B009FM6P6M?tag=spankyspinola-20), writes profoundly about how trauma is passed down through generations, not just psychologically, but physically, in the very structure of our nervous systems. He speaks of 'white body supremacy' and how the trauma of oppression is held in the bodies of both the oppressed and the oppressors, and those who are forced to mediate between them.

As a government worker, you are often the face of the state. You are the interface between the individual and the institution. This places you in a deeply complex somatic position. You are absorbing the trauma of the people you serve, and you are also absorbing the structural violence of the institution you represent. Your body becomes a crossroads of historical and systemic forces. The tension in your gut is not just about the angry phone call you had this morning; it is about the centuries of injustice that created the conditions for that phone call.

Menakem teaches that we must heal this trauma in the body, through somatic practices that allow the nervous system to settle and process the accumulated charge. We cannot simply legislate our way out of this. We cannot policy-make our way out of this. We must feel our way out of this. We must allow the body to shake, to cry, to rage, to release the energy that has been trapped in the tissues. This is not a comfortable process. It is messy, it is loud, and it is deeply necessary.

When you sit at your desk, feeling the familiar tightening in your chest, remember that you are part of a lineage. You are part of a long line of people who have witnessed suffering and felt the imperative to respond. Your rage is a testament to your humanity. It is a sign that your heart is still beating, that your empathy has not been entirely extinguished by the bureaucracy. Do not pathologize your anger. Honor it. It is the fire that keeps you human in a system designed to turn you into a machine.

## The Practice of Embodied Refusal

How do you survive this? How do you continue to do the work without letting the work destroy you? You must develop a practice of embodied refusal. This does not mean quitting your job, though that is always an option. It means refusing to let the institution colonize your internal landscape. It means maintaining a sovereign territory within your own skin, a place where the rules of the bureaucracy do not apply.

This requires daily, intentional practice. It requires tuning in to the subtle signals of your body and responding to them with care and respect. It means noticing when your breathing becomes shallow and deliberately taking a deep, belly breath. It means noticing when your shoulders creep up to your ears and consciously dropping them. It means taking up space in a system that wants you to be small and compliant.

Here is a short embodied practice for the public servant:

1. **Find your feet.** Sit in your government-issued chair. Take off your shoes if you can. Place your bare feet flat on the floor. Feel the solid ground beneath you. The building may be owned by the state, but the earth beneath it belongs to no one. Connect with that earth.

2. **Locate the rage.** Close your eyes. Scan your body. Where is the anger living right now? Is it a hot coal in your stomach? A tight band around your chest? A buzzing in your jaw? Do not try to change it. Just locate it. Acknowledge it.

3. **Breathe into the tension.** Direct your breath to the area where the rage is concentrated. Imagine your breath as a cool, spacious wind, creating room around the tight, hot sensation. You are not trying to blow the anger away; you are trying to give it space to exist without consuming you.

4. **The micro-shake.** Allow the part of your body holding the tension to tremble slightly. If it's your jaw, let your lower jaw vibrate. If it's your hands, let your fingers twitch. This is the nervous system discharging the trapped energy of the fight-or-flight response. Let it happen. It might feel strange, but it is deeply biological.

5. **Return to the room.** Open your eyes. Look around your cubicle. Notice three objects that are not related to your work - a plant, a photograph, a crack in the wall. Reorient yourself to the present moment. You are here. You are alive. You are more than your job description.

## The Illusion of the Neutral Observer

The bureaucracy demands neutrality. It demands that you process the eviction notice with the same emotional detachment as you process the permit for a new deck. It demands that you treat human tragedy as a data point. But the body does not understand neutrality. The body only understands safety and threat, connection and isolation, nourishment and starvation.

When you force yourself to be neutral in the face of suffering, you are committing an act of violence against your own nervous system. You are severing the connection between your heart and your face, between your gut and your voice. This severance is the root of burnout. Burnout is not just being tired; it is the collapse of the internal architecture that sustains empathy. It is the body saying, 'I can no longer hold the contradiction between what I feel and what I am required to perform.'

Gabor Maté, a physician who has spent his life studying the connection between stress, trauma, and illness, argues that the suppression of authentic emotion is a primary driver of chronic disease. In his work, he details how the constant pressure to conform to societal or institutional expectations forces us to abandon our authentic selves, leading to a state of chronic physiological stress. The body, unable to express its truth through emotion or action, expresses it through pathology. The autoimmune disease, the chronic pain, the severe depression - these are not random malfunctions; they are the body's desperate attempt to say 'no' when the mouth is forced to say 'yes.'

As a government worker, your 'no' is constantly suppressed. You cannot say 'no' to the policy. You cannot say 'no' to the angry citizen. You cannot say 'no' to the crushing workload. So your body says 'no' for you. It gives you a migraine so severe you cannot look at the screen. It gives you a panic attack in the parking lot. It gives you a fatigue so profound that getting out of bed feels like moving through wet sand. Listen to the 'no.' It is not a weakness. It is a profound biological intelligence trying to save your life.

## Reclaiming the Fire

Anger is a messenger. It tells us that a boundary has been crossed, that an injustice has occurred, that something needs to change. When we suppress our anger, we shoot the messenger, but the message remains, festering in the dark. To heal the somatic wounds of public service, we must invite the messenger back in. We must learn to listen to our anger without being consumed by it.

This means finding safe containers for the rage. It means finding spaces where you can speak the unspeakable, where you can name the cruelty of the system without fear of reprisal. It means connecting with other public servants who understand the specific, agonizing contours of this work. It means engaging in somatic practices that allow the physical energy of the anger to move through you - vigorous exercise, screaming into a pillow, dancing wildly in your living room, chopping wood.

It also means recognizing the limits of your own agency. You cannot fix the system alone. You cannot save everyone who comes to your desk. You are a human being, not a savior. You have a finite amount of energy, a finite capacity for bearing witness. You must fiercely protect your own life force. You must build a life outside of the institution that is rich, nourishing, and deeply connected to the natural world.

The system wants to consume you. It wants to extract your empathy, your labor, and your life force, and give you a pension in return. Do not let it. Keep your fire. Keep your rage. Let it fuel your commitment to your own humanity. Let it remind you that you are alive, that you care, that you are part of a web of connection that is older and stronger than any government agency. You are a somatic being, a creature of earth and water, of blood and bone. Reclaim your body. Reclaim your fire.

The physical toll of this work is often invisible to those outside the system. They see the benefits, the pension, the perceived stability. They do not see the cost extracted from your very cells. They do not see the way your digestion slows to a crawl because your body is perpetually in a state of sympathetic arousal, diverting blood flow away from the organs of rest and repair and toward the muscles of action. But there is no action to take. You are trapped behind a desk, your body screaming to run or fight, while you politely ask for a signature on line 14. This biological contradiction is the engine of your exhaustion. It is a slow, quiet violence enacted upon your physiology day after day, year after year.

Consider the diaphragm, the primary muscle of respiration. In a state of safety, the diaphragm moves fluidly, massaging the vagus nerve and signaling to the brain that all is well. But in the chronic stress of the bureaucratic environment, the diaphragm becomes tight and restricted. Your breathing becomes shallow, confined to the upper chest. This shallow breathing sends a continuous feedback loop of alarm to the brain, reinforcing the state of hypervigilance. You are literally suffocating under the weight of the institution. Somatic practices that focus on restoring the full, natural excursion of the diaphragm are not just relaxation techniques; they are essential interventions for interrupting the cycle of chronic trauma.

We must also speak of the eyes. The eyes are not just organs of vision; they are extensions of the brain, intimately connected to the nervous system. Staring at a screen for eight hours a day, processing endless streams of data, forces the eyes into a narrow, fixed focus. This narrow focus is the visual equivalent of the fight-or-flight response. It signals to the nervous system that there is a threat directly ahead that requires intense concentration. To counter this, you must practice panoramic vision. Look out a window. Let your gaze soften and expand to take in the periphery. This simple act of shifting your visual field can profoundly down-regulate the nervous system, signaling to the body that the immediate threat has passed and it is safe to widen your perspective.

The work of the public servant is often described as a calling, a noble sacrifice for the greater good. But sacrifice implies a willing offering, a conscious choice to give something of value for a higher purpose. What happens in the bureaucracy is often not sacrifice, but extraction. The system extracts your empathy, your patience, your physical health, without your full, informed consent. It relies on your inherent desire to help, weaponizing your compassion against you. Recognizing this dynamic is a crucial step in somatic reclamation. You must learn to distinguish between genuine service, which nourishes both the giver and the receiver, and institutional extraction, which leaves you hollowed out and depleted.

This is why the integration of named teachers and specific somatic tools is so vital. We are not inventing this wheel. We are drawing on deep wells of wisdom, from the ancient traditions of herbalism and Traditional Chinese Medicine to the modern insights of neurobiology and trauma resolution. When Thich Nhat Hanh speaks of mindfulness, he is not talking about a detached, floating state of bliss; he is talking about the fierce, grounded presence required to face suffering without turning away. He is talking about the capacity to hold the pain of the world in your heart while keeping your feet firmly planted on the earth. This is the ultimate somatic practice for the public servant: to remain present, to remain feeling, to remain human, in a system that demands your mechanization.

## The Slow Work of Somatic Repair

Repairing the damage done by years of institutional holding is not a quick fix. It is not a weekend retreat or a single cup of tea. It is a slow, deliberate process of re-patterning the nervous system. It is the work of teaching the body that it is safe to soften, safe to feel, safe to release the armor it has worn for so long.

This work requires patience. The fascia that has tightened around your heart did not become rigid overnight, and it will not release overnight. You must approach your body with the gentleness and respect you would offer a frightened animal. You must learn to track the subtle shifts in your internal state, noticing when the tension begins to build and intervening before it becomes a full-blown somatic lockdown.

Consider the practice of titration, a concept from Somatic Experiencing, developed by Peter Levine. Titration involves exposing the nervous system to small, manageable amounts of stress or trauma, and then allowing it to settle and integrate before introducing more. In the context of public service, this might mean taking micro-breaks throughout the day to consciously relax your jaw and drop your shoulders. It might mean setting a timer to remind yourself to take three deep breaths every hour. It might mean choosing to disengage from a toxic conversation in the breakroom and instead walking outside to feel the wind on your face.

These small acts of somatic rebellion accumulate over time. They build a reservoir of resilience in the nervous system. They create a buffer between you and the relentless demands of the institution. They remind your body that it has choices, that it is not entirely at the mercy of the bureaucracy. This is the slow, quiet work of reclaiming your sovereignty, one breath, one muscle, one moment at a time.

## FAQ

**Q: I feel guilty when I take time for myself because the people I serve are in such desperate need. How do I reconcile this?**

**A:** Your guilt is a symptom of the system's dysfunction, not a reflection of your moral failing. The institution relies on your boundless empathy to compensate for its structural inadequacies. When you sacrifice your own well-being to fill the gaps left by a broken system, you are not fixing the system; you are enabling it. Your primary responsibility is to your own organism. A depleted, dysregulated nervous system cannot offer genuine support to anyone. Taking care of your body is not a luxury; it is a biological imperative and a radical act of resistance against a culture of extraction.

**Q: My supervisor says I need to have a more 'positive attitude.' How do I handle this without losing my job?**

**A:** The demand for a 'positive attitude' in the face of systemic cruelty is a form of gaslighting. It is an attempt to police your emotional reality and force you to perform compliance. You do not have to change your internal reality to satisfy their demand. You can learn to perform the necessary bureaucratic functions while maintaining your internal sovereignty. Practice the 'gray rock' method: be polite, professional, and entirely uninteresting. Do not offer your authentic emotional energy to people or institutions that have not earned it. Save your fire for the spaces and people that nourish you.

**Q: I've been doing this work for twenty years, and my body feels like a block of concrete. Is it too late to heal?**

**A:** It is never too late. The human nervous system is remarkably plastic, meaning it has the capacity to change and reorganize itself throughout our entire lifespan. The fascia can be hydrated and softened. The neural pathways of chronic stress can be re-routed. It will take time, and it will require consistent, gentle practice, but your body wants to heal. It is always moving toward homeostasis, toward balance. You just have to provide the right conditions - safety, connection, and somatic support - and trust the deep, evolutionary wisdom of your own tissues.

**Q: Are herbs really strong enough to counter the stress of a government job?**

**A:** Herbs are not magic bullets, and they will not fix a broken bureaucracy. But they are powerful allies in the work of somatic repair. They provide the biochemical support your nervous system needs to process stress and return to a state of balance. Think of them as nutritional support for your resilience. When combined with somatic practices, boundaries, and a commitment to your own well-being, herbs can profoundly shift your physical and emotional landscape. They remind your body that it is supported by the earth, even when it is being crushed by the state.
